title: 2  freq_spss
text:
2.2 freq_spss

2.2.1 What the function does
freq_spss() produces a frequency table for a labelled survey variable, modelled on the output style of SPSS. It is designed for use with haven-imported datasets, where variables carry value labels, variable labels, and user-defined missing values (user NAs) — metadata that ordinary R frequency tools ignore.
The output distinguishes three kinds of rows:

Valid responses — cases with a recognised, non-missing value. These rows show frequency, percentage of valid cases, and cumulative percentage within valid cases.
User-defined missing values — codes the researcher has flagged as missing (e.g. “Don’t know”, “Refused”) but which are tracked separately rather than silently dropped. These rows show frequency only.
Total columns — percentage and cumulative percentage computed across all rows (valid + user NAs combined), replicating the “Total %” column familiar from SPSS output.

The result is either a plain tibble (for downstream manipulation) or a formatted flextable (for Word reports or RMarkdown output), depending on the as_flextable argument.



2.2.2 How the code works

2.2.2.1 1. Input validation and setup
The function begins with a simple data frame check, then extracts the target variable as a plain vector using pull(). A descriptive column label is constructed by combining the R variable name with the haven variable label (var_label()).


2.2.2.2 2. Three factor views
The variable is converted into factors three times, each serving a different purpose:








Object
Contents
How produced




var_all
All values including user NAs
to_factor(levels = "prefixed")


var_no_NA
Valid values only
to_factor(..., user_na_to_na = TRUE)


var_only_NA
User-NA values only
Subset raw variable to na_values attribute, then convert



The levels = "prefixed" argument produces labels in the form [1] Yes, which preserves the numeric code alongside the label text.


2.2.2.3 3. Three sub-tables, then joined
Rather than a single computation, the table is assembled from three separate tibbles that are then joined:

valid_tbl — counts and proportions of non-missing cases, with cumulative % computed at this stage (so it runs only over valid rows).
user_na_tbl — counts only, for user-NA rows.
total_tbl — proportions across all rows combined.

These are joined sequentially with explicit full_join(..., by = "f") calls. The n and type columns that collide across joins are resolved with coalesce(). Rows are then arrange()d to put valid cases first before cumsum() computes the total cumulative percentage, ensuring stable ordering.


2.2.2.4 4. Label deduplication
Haven’s prefix format ([1] Yes) can produce redundant labels for unlabelled numeric variables, where a value like 1 gets labelled [1] 1. The function detects this by splitting each level on ] and comparing the left (value) and right (label) parts. Where they match, the prefix is stripped. A deduplication step guards against duplicate names in the recoding vector, which would otherwise trigger a warning from fct_recode().


2.2.2.5 5. Output
The type column and the generic f column name are tidied up — f is replaced by the descriptive variable label. If as_flextable = TRUE, the tibble is passed to an internal helper .freq_format_table(), which applies row shading (blue for valid, grey for user NAs), human-readable column headers, right-alignment, and vertical dividers.
Requires: dplyr, forcats, stringr, purrr, labelled, flextable

#' Generate an SPSS-style frequency table for a labelled variable
#'
#' @param data        A data frame containing the variable of interest.
#' @param var         The (unquoted) variable to tabulate.
#' @param as_flextable Logical. If TRUE, returns a formatted flextable;
#'                    otherwise returns a plain tibble. Default: FALSE.
#' @return A tibble or flextable with frequency, percentage, cumulative %,
#'         and totals, with user-defined missing values distinguished from
#'         valid values.

freq_spss <- function(data, var, as_flextable = FALSE) {
  
  # ── Input validation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  if (!is.data.frame(data)) stop("`data` must be a data frame.")
  
  # ── 1. Extract the raw variable and build a descriptive column label ──────────
  variable <- data %>% dplyr::pull({{var}})
  label <- paste0(
    data %>% dplyr::select({{var}}) %>% names,
    " - ",
    labelled::var_label(variable)
  )
  
  # ── 2. Build three views of the variable as factors ───────────────────────────
  var_all <- variable %>% labelled::to_factor(levels = "prefixed") %>% forcats::fct_drop()
  
  var_no_NA <- variable %>% labelled::to_factor(levels = "prefixed", user_na_to_na = TRUE) %>% forcats::fct_drop()
  
  user_na_vals <- attr(variable, "na_values")
  
  if (is.null(user_na_vals) || length(user_na_vals) == 0) {
    var_only_NA <- factor(character(0))
  } else {
    var_only_NA <- variable[variable %in% user_na_vals] %>%
      labelled::to_factor(levels = "prefixed") %>%
      forcats::fct_drop()
  }
  
  # ── 3. Build the frequency table by joining three sub-tables ──────────────────
  valid_tbl <- var_no_NA %>%
    stats::na.omit() %>%
    forcats::fct_count(prop = TRUE) %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(f)) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      cum_p = paste0(round(cumsum(p) * 100, 2), "%"),
      p     = paste0(round(p * 100, 2), "%"),
      type  = "valid"
    )
  
  user_na_tbl <- var_only_NA %>%
    forcats::fct_count(prop = FALSE) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(type = "user_na")
  
  total_tbl <- var_all %>%
    forcats::fct_count(prop = TRUE) %>%
    dplyr::select(-n) %>%
    dplyr::rename(total_p = p)
  
  table <- valid_tbl %>%
    dplyr::full_join(user_na_tbl, by = "f") %>%
    dplyr::full_join(total_tbl,   by = "f") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      n    = dplyr::coalesce(n.x, n.y),
      type = dplyr::coalesce(type.x, type.y)
    ) %>%
    dplyr::select(-n.x, -n.y, -type.x, -type.y) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(desc(type == "valid"), f) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      cum_p_total = paste0(round(cumsum(total_p) * 100, 2), "%"),
      total_p     = paste0(round(total_p * 100, 2), "%")
    ) %>%
    dplyr::select(type, f, n, p, cum_p, total_p, cum_p_total) 
  
  # ── 4. Clean up factor level labels ───────────────────────────────────────────
  labels_full          <- table %>% dplyr::pull(f)
  max_break_appearance <- labels_full %>% as.character() %>% stringr::str_count("]") %>% max
  
  if (max_break_appearance == 1 && length(labels_full) > 0){
    splits     <- labels_full %>% stringr::str_split("] ", n = 2)
    labels     <- purrr::map_chr(splits, ~if (length(.x) >= 2) .x[2] else .x[1])
    value_part <- purrr::map_chr(splits, ~.x[1]) %>% stringr::str_remove("\\[")
    match      <- purrr::map2_lgl(labels, value_part, ~.x == .y)
    
    recoding        <- labels_full %>% as.character()
    new_names       <- dplyr::case_when(match ~ labels, !match ~ as.character(labels_full))
    names(recoding) <- new_names
    recoding        <- recoding[!duplicated(names(recoding))]
    
    table <- table %>% dplyr::mutate(f = forcats::fct_recode(f, !!!recoding))
  }
  
  names(table)[2] <- label
  
  # ── 5. Optional flextable formatting ─────────────────────────────────────────
  if (isTRUE(as_flextable)) {
    table <- .freq_format_table(table)
  }
  
  return(table)
}


#' Internal helper: apply flextable formatting to a freq_spss tibble
#'
#' @param tbl A tibble returned by the core freq_spss logic (before column rename).
#' @return A formatted flextable object.
#' @keywords internal

.freq_format_table <- function(tbl) {
  tbl %>%
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::autofit() %>%
    flextable::bg(i = ~type == "user_na", j = NULL, bg = "lightgrey") %>%
    flextable::bg(i = ~type != "user_na", j = NULL, bg = "lightblue") %>%
    flextable::bg(part = "header",                  bg = "lightgrey") %>%
    flextable::delete_columns("type") %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(
      n           = "Frequency",
      p           = "Percentage",
      cum_p       = "Cumulative %",
      total_p     = "Total %",
      cum_p_total = "Total Cumulative %"
    ) %>%
    flextable::align(j = -1, align = "right", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::vline(j = c(1, 2, 4), part = "all")
}

title: 3  Frequency function for SPSS
text:
3.2 freq_spss

3.2.1 What the function does
freq_spss() produces a frequency table for a labelled survey variable, modelled on the output style of SPSS. It is designed for use with haven-imported datasets, where variables carry value labels, variable labels, and user-defined missing values (user NAs) — metadata that ordinary R frequency tools ignore.
The output distinguishes three kinds of rows:

Valid responses — cases with a recognised, non-missing value. These rows show frequency, percentage of valid cases, and cumulative percentage within valid cases.
User-defined missing values — codes the researcher has flagged as missing (e.g. “Don’t know”, “Refused”) but which are tracked separately rather than silently dropped. These rows show frequency only.
Total columns — percentage and cumulative percentage computed across all rows (valid + user NAs combined), replicating the “Total %” column familiar from SPSS output.

The result is either a plain tibble (for downstream manipulation) or a formatted flextable (for Word reports or RMarkdown output), depending on the as_flextable argument.



3.2.2 How the code works

3.2.2.1 1. Input validation and setup
The function begins with a simple data frame check, then extracts the target variable as a plain vector using pull(). A descriptive column label is constructed by combining the R variable name with the haven variable label (var_label()).


3.2.2.2 2. Three factor views
The variable is converted into factors three times, each serving a different purpose:








Object
Contents
How produced




var_all
All values including user NAs
to_factor(levels = "prefixed")


var_no_NA
Valid values only
to_factor(..., user_na_to_na = TRUE)


var_only_NA
User-NA values only
Subset raw variable to na_values attribute, then convert



The levels = "prefixed" argument produces labels in the form [1] Yes, which preserves the numeric code alongside the label text.


3.2.2.3 3. Three sub-tables, then joined
Rather than a single computation, the table is assembled from three separate tibbles that are then joined:

valid_tbl — counts and proportions of non-missing cases, with cumulative % computed at this stage (so it runs only over valid rows).
user_na_tbl — counts only, for user-NA rows.
total_tbl — proportions across all rows combined.

These are joined sequentially with explicit full_join(..., by = "f") calls. The n and type columns that collide across joins are resolved with coalesce(). Rows are then arrange()d to put valid cases first before cumsum() computes the total cumulative percentage, ensuring stable ordering.


3.2.2.4 4. Label deduplication
Haven’s prefix format ([1] Yes) can produce redundant labels for unlabelled numeric variables, where a value like 1 gets labelled [1] 1. The function detects this by splitting each level on ] and comparing the left (value) and right (label) parts. Where they match, the prefix is stripped. A deduplication step guards against duplicate names in the recoding vector, which would otherwise trigger a warning from fct_recode().


3.2.2.5 5. Output
The type column and the generic f column name are tidied up — f is replaced by the descriptive variable label. If as_flextable = TRUE, the tibble is passed to an internal helper .freq_format_table(), which applies row shading (blue for valid, grey for user NAs), human-readable column headers, right-alignment, and vertical dividers.
Requires: dplyr, forcats, stringr, purrr, labelled, flextable

#' Generate an SPSS-style frequency table for a labelled variable
#'
#' @param data        A data frame containing the variable of interest.
#' @param var         The (unquoted) variable to tabulate.
#' @param as_flextable Logical. If TRUE, returns a formatted flextable;
#'                    otherwise returns a plain tibble. Default: FALSE.
#' @return A tibble or flextable with frequency, percentage, cumulative %,
#'         and totals, with user-defined missing values distinguished from
#'         valid values.

freq_spss <- function(data, var, as_flextable = FALSE) {
  
  # ── Input validation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  if (!is.data.frame(data)) stop("`data` must be a data frame.")
  
  # ── 1. Extract the raw variable and build a descriptive column label ──────────
  variable <- data %>% dplyr::pull({{var}})
  label <- paste0(
    data %>% dplyr::select({{var}}) %>% names,
    " - ",
    labelled::var_label(variable)
  )
  
  # ── 2. Build three views of the variable as factors ───────────────────────────
  var_all <- variable %>% labelled::to_factor(levels = "prefixed") %>% forcats::fct_drop()
  
  var_no_NA <- variable %>% labelled::to_factor(levels = "prefixed", user_na_to_na = TRUE) %>% forcats::fct_drop()
  
  user_na_vals <- attr(variable, "na_values")
  
  if (is.null(user_na_vals) || length(user_na_vals) == 0) {
    var_only_NA <- factor(character(0))
  } else {
    var_only_NA <- variable[variable %in% user_na_vals] %>%
      labelled::to_factor(levels = "prefixed") %>%
      forcats::fct_drop()
  }
  
  # ── 3. Build the frequency table by joining three sub-tables ──────────────────
  valid_tbl <- var_no_NA %>%
    stats::na.omit() %>%
    forcats::fct_count(prop = TRUE) %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(f)) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      cum_p = paste0(round(cumsum(p) * 100, 2), "%"),
      p     = paste0(round(p * 100, 2), "%"),
      type  = "valid"
    )
  
  user_na_tbl <- var_only_NA %>%
    forcats::fct_count(prop = FALSE) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(type = "user_na")
  
  total_tbl <- var_all %>%
    forcats::fct_count(prop = TRUE) %>%
    dplyr::select(-n) %>%
    dplyr::rename(total_p = p)
  
  table <- valid_tbl %>%
    dplyr::full_join(user_na_tbl, by = "f") %>%
    dplyr::full_join(total_tbl,   by = "f") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      n    = dplyr::coalesce(n.x, n.y),
      type = dplyr::coalesce(type.x, type.y)
    ) %>%
    dplyr::select(-n.x, -n.y, -type.x, -type.y) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(desc(type == "valid"), f) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      cum_p_total = paste0(round(cumsum(total_p) * 100, 2), "%"),
      total_p     = paste0(round(total_p * 100, 2), "%")
    ) %>%
    dplyr::select(type, f, n, p, cum_p, total_p, cum_p_total) 
  
  # ── 4. Clean up factor level labels ───────────────────────────────────────────
  labels_full          <- table %>% dplyr::pull(f)
  max_break_appearance <- labels_full %>% as.character() %>% stringr::str_count("]") %>% max
  
  if (max_break_appearance == 1 && length(labels_full) > 0){
    splits     <- labels_full %>% stringr::str_split("] ", n = 2)
    labels     <- purrr::map_chr(splits, ~if (length(.x) >= 2) .x[2] else .x[1])
    value_part <- purrr::map_chr(splits, ~.x[1]) %>% stringr::str_remove("\\[")
    match      <- purrr::map2_lgl(labels, value_part, ~.x == .y)
    
    recoding        <- labels_full %>% as.character()
    new_names       <- dplyr::case_when(match ~ labels, !match ~ as.character(labels_full))
    names(recoding) <- new_names
    recoding        <- recoding[!duplicated(names(recoding))]
    
    table <- table %>% dplyr::mutate(f = forcats::fct_recode(f, !!!recoding))
  }
  
  names(table)[2] <- label
  
  # ── 5. Optional flextable formatting ─────────────────────────────────────────
  if (isTRUE(as_flextable)) {
    table <- .freq_format_table(table)
  }
  
  return(table)
}


#' Internal helper: apply flextable formatting to a freq_spss tibble
#'
#' @param tbl A tibble returned by the core freq_spss logic (before column rename).
#' @return A formatted flextable object.
#' @keywords internal

.freq_format_table <- function(tbl) {
  tbl %>%
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::autofit() %>%
    flextable::bg(i = ~type == "user_na", j = NULL, bg = "lightgrey") %>%
    flextable::bg(i = ~type != "user_na", j = NULL, bg = "lightblue") %>%
    flextable::bg(part = "header",                  bg = "lightgrey") %>%
    flextable::delete_columns("type") %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(
      n           = "Frequency",
      p           = "Percentage",
      cum_p       = "Cumulative %",
      total_p     = "Total %",
      cum_p_total = "Total Cumulative %"
    ) %>%
    flextable::align(j = -1, align = "right", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::vline(j = c(1, 2, 4), part = "all")
}

2.2 unique_vals

unique_vals <- function(data, max_values = 30){
  
  vect <- 
    data %>% 
    map(., ~unique(to_factor(.x))) %>% 
    map(., function(xxx) {
      
      vec_length <- length(xxx)
      if(vec_length > max_values){vec_length <- max_values}
      result <- paste0(xxx[1:vec_length], collapse = ", ")
      return(result)
      
      }) %>%
     as_tibble %>% 
    pivot_longer(cols= everything())
  
  count_unique <- data %>% 
    map(., ~length(unique(to_factor(.x)))) %>% 
    as_tibble() %>% 
    pivot_longer(cols = everything()) %>% 
    rename(n_unique = value)
  
  vect <- full_join(count_unique, vect, by = "name") 
  names(vect)[3] <- paste("First", max_values, "unique values")
  
  return(vect)

}

title: 2  Data dictionary
text:
2.3 df_count

df_count <- function(data) { 
    map(names(data), ~data %>% 
          pull(all_of(.x)) %>% 
          to_factor() %>% 
          fct_count() %>% 
          mutate(name = .x, .before = 1) %>% 
          rename(levels = f) %>% 
          filter(n != 0) %>%
          mutate(id = row_number()) %>%
          filter(id %in% 1:10) %>% 
          mutate(Count = paste0(levels, ";  N = ", n)) %>% 
          select(-id, -n, -levels)
        ) %>% 
      bind_rows 
}

title: 2  Data dictionary
text:
2.4 fullsome

fullsome <- function(data, max_values = 30, count = F, unique_vals = T){
  
  components <- list(
  
    map(data, ~sum(is.na(.x))) %>% as_tibble() %>% pivot_longer(cols = everything()) %>% rename(n_miss = value) %>% mutate(p_miss = paste0(round(n_miss / nrow(data) * 100, 2), "%")),
    
    map(data, ~paste0(class(.x), collapse = ", ")) %>% as_tibble() %>% pivot_longer(cols = everything()) %>% rename(class = value),
    
    map(data, ~var_label(.x)) %>% as_tibble() %>% pivot_longer(cols = everything()) %>% rename(label = value)
    
    )
  
  if(unique_vals == T){
    components <- append(components, list(unique_vals(data, max_values = max_values)))
  }
  
  results <- reduce(components, full_join, by = "name") %>% 
    relocate(name, label, any_of("n_unique"), n_miss, p_miss, class)
  
    if (count == T) {
    
    counts <- data %>% df_count()
    results <- full_join(results, counts, by = "name")
        
  }
  
  return(results)
  
}

title: 2  look_for2
text:
2.3 Display look_for

tester <- look_for(df, details = FALSE)

look_h <- function(data, 
                   print = "h", ## set to f for flextable
                   capt = NULL,  ## Set to string to use as caption. 
                   export = NULL ## Set to file location
                   ){ 
  
  ht <- 
    
    data |>
  
    huxtable::as_hux() %>%
    
    ## Applied to all cells
    ## amount of space on each side of a cell. 
    huxtable::set_all_padding(1) |> 
    ## amount of space on each side of a cell. 
    huxtable::set_outer_padding(1) |>
    ## decimal place display (works on all numbers found in a cell, even non-numeric data)
    huxtable::set_number_format(2) |> 
    ## Font size
    huxtable::set_font_size(8) |> 
    ## Font
    huxtable::set_font("Arial") |>
    
    ## Set zero decimal places
    huxtable::set_number_format(everywhere, value = fmt_pretty(0)) %>%
    
    ## Heading formatting
    ## bold top row (the column names)
    huxtable::set_bold(row = 1, col = everywhere) |> 
    ## Add bottom border of row 1, default value = 0.4 points.
    huxtable::set_bottom_border(row = 1, col = everywhere) |>  
  
    ## Right hand border to variable column
    huxtable::set_right_border(everywhere, ## all rows
                     2,  ## column 2 (variable name)
                     brdr(2, "solid", "grey")) |> 
    
    ## Set NA string
    huxtable::set_na_string("-") |>
  
    ## External borders only... 
    huxtable::set_outer_borders(everywhere, everywhere, brdr(1,style = "solid", "black")) |>
    
    ## Background colour in even rows to grey, all columns
    huxtable::set_background_color(evens, everywhere, "grey95") |>
    
    ## Right align first column
    huxtable::set_align(everywhere, everywhere, "left") |>
    
    ## Wrap columns (only works when width set to (default is TRUE anyway))
    huxtable::set_wrap(everywhere, everywhere, TRUE) |>
    
    ## Width of the table as a proportion of the available space - here 100%
    huxtable::set_width(1)
    
    ## Add table caption
    if(!is.null(capt)){ht <- ht |> huxtable::set_caption(capt)} 
    
    ## Control column widths, dynamic based on content
    col_lengths <- apply(ht, 2, function(col) max(nchar(as.character(col))))
    relative_widths <- col_lengths / sum(col_lengths)
    huxtable::col_width(ht) <- relative_widths
    
    ## Export to Excel? 
    if (!is.null(export)){
        
        wb <- huxtable::as_Workbook(ht = ht, sheet = "lookfor")
        ## Excel cell locations
        end_row <- 1 + nrow(data) ## 1 here because huxtable adds header row to number of rows. 
        end_col <- ncol(data)
        ## Required to remove row heights set by as_Workbook() - these are problematic
        removeRowHeights(wb, "lookfor", rows = 2:end_row) 
        
        addStyle(wb, 
             "lookfor", 
             style = createStyle(wrapText = T), 
             cols = 2:end_col, 
             rows = 2:end_row, 
             gridExpand = TRUE, 
             stack = T)
    
        ## Remove huxtable defined column widths and apply openxlsx standards
        removeColWidths(wb, "lookfor", cols = 2:end_col)
        
        ## Temporary function to identify column width
        .col_width <- function(huxt) { 
      
            ## Extract first row of huxtable
            header_row_char_length <- 1:ncol(huxt) %>% map(., ~as_tibble(huxt[,.x])) %>% map_int(., ~max(str_length(as_vector(.x))))
          
            ## Multiple by number of pixels per character
            header_proposed_width <- header_row_char_length + 1
          
            ## Cut off of 30 imposed, and lower bound of 10. 
            header_proposed_width_cut_off <- ifelse(header_proposed_width > 50, 50, header_proposed_width)
            header_proposed_width_cut_off <- ifelse(header_proposed_width_cut_off < 10, 10, header_proposed_width_cut_off)
            
          return(header_proposed_width_cut_off)
          
          }
        
        col_widths <- ht %>% .col_width()
        setColWidths(wb, "lookfor", cols = 1:end_col, widths = col_widths)
        saveWorkbook(wb, file = export, overwrite = TRUE)
    }
    
    
    if(print == "f"){ht <- ht %>% huxtable::as_flextable() %>% flextable::autofit()}
    
    return(ht)
    
}

title: 2  look_for2
text:
2.4 look_for with a search filter

look_for2 <- function(data, 
                      filter, 
                      details = "med", ## high or low
                      form = F,  ## if T then format
                      type = "f" ## set "h" for huxtable output
                      ){
  
  results <-
      look_for(data) %>%
      convert_list_columns_to_character() %>%
      filter(grepl(filter, variable, ignore.case = T) |
             grepl(filter, label, ignore.case = T) |
             grepl(filter, levels, ignore.case = T) |
             grepl(filter, value_labels, ignore.case = T)
             ) 
  
  if(details == "med"){
    results <- results %>% select(pos, variable, label, levels, value_labels)
  } else if (details == "low") {
    results <- results %>% select(pos, variable, label)
  } else if (details == "high") {
    results <- results %>% select(pos, variable, label)
  }
  
  if(form == T){
    results <- results %>% look_h(print = type, capt = paste0("Search for: ", filter))
  }
    
  return(results)
  
}
